title: Applying shellac sanding sealer before varnishing or painting
subtitle: Seal bare or stripped timber with shellac to lock in resin, knots, and stain bleed before a topcoat
excerpt: How to apply a shellac-based sanding sealer or knotting solution to bare timber before varnishing or painting. Covers when shellac is needed, the dilution and brush technique, sanding between coats, and how it prevents resin bleed and stain contamination.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 6150: Code of practice for painting of buildings. Shellac finishing technique; traditional woodworking practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Frederick Miller, Wood Finishing and Refinishing (1988).

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural resin secreted by the lac insect and dissolved in methylated spirit (denatured alcohol). Dries by solvent evaporation in 20 to 30 minutes. Used as a sanding sealer (a slightly gritty first coat that can be sanded smooth), a knotting solution (for resin and knot sealing), and a barrier coat between incompatible finishes. Shellac is not water-resistant and should not be used as a final finish on surfaces exposed to water or alcohol.

A coat of shellac applied between two otherwise incompatible finishes — for example, between an oil-based stain and a water-based varnish. Without a barrier coat, the water in the varnish can reactivate an oil-based stain and cause it to bleed upward through the varnish. Shellac dries completely neutral and accepts any topcoat — oil-based, water-based, or solvent-based — applied over it.

content.text: Shellac is the universal barrier coat in wood finishing. A thin coat of
over bare timber before varnishing seals knots and resin pockets so they cannot bleed through. A coat of shellac between a stain and a water-based topcoat acts as a
, stopping the stain from reactivating and muddying the topcoat. Used as a sanding sealer (the primary use in furniture restoration), it gives the topcoat a uniform, low-porosity surface to key into.

Shellac sanding sealer (sold by Rustins, Liberon, and general decorators merchants; also sold as 'knotting solution' for the concentrated version). Methylated spirit for diluting and for brush cleaning. A 25 mm fine-bristle brush. 240-grit and 320-grit sanding paper. A sanding block.

Apply shellac before any topcoat in four situations. First, when the timber has knots or resin streaks that bleed through paint. Second, when a stain has been applied before a water-based topcoat. Third, when two incompatible products need to be separated. Fourth, when the timber is very porous, such as oak, ash, or mahogany.
Step 2, apply the first shellac coat

Dilute the shellac with methylated spirit by 20% for the first coat; this gives a thinner coat that penetrates well. Apply quickly with the brush in long strokes along the grain. Shellac dries very fast (touch-dry in 15 to 20 minutes at room temperature) and cannot be re-brushed once it has started to skin; work in one direction without stopping. A thin even coat is the target; a thick coat runs and is difficult to sand.
Step 3, sand and apply a second coat

Allow the first coat to dry for 20 to 30 minutes. Sand lightly with 240-grit along the grain; the shellac raises the grain slightly and may have a slightly rough surface from dust or brush marks. Wipe off the dust with a clean cloth. Apply the second coat at full strength. Sand with 320-grit after this coat. Two shellac coats are sufficient as a sealer; the topcoat goes on over the sanded second coat.

Any topcoat goes over dry shellac: oil-based varnish, water-based varnish, lacquer, or paint. Allow a full hour before applying any water-based topcoat. A shorter wait risks the water in the topcoat reactivating the shellac and causing adhesion problems.

attrs.symptom: Shellac drying with a white, cloudy bloom
attrs.cause: Applied in a humid environment. Shellac dissolved in methylated spirit is sensitive to ambient moisture.
attrs.fix: Apply shellac only in dry conditions (relative humidity below 70%). The bloom is in the shellac film; once it has dried fully, light sanding usually removes the cloudiness.

attrs.symptom: Topcoat peeling off the shellac-sealed surface within weeks
attrs.cause: Shellac not fully cured before the topcoat was applied, or the shellac surface was contaminated with oil.
attrs.fix: Remove the peeling topcoat. Clean the shellac surface with a cloth dampened with methylated spirit. Allow to dry and apply fresh shellac. Apply the topcoat only when the shellac has cured for at least 1 hour.
